modules.title: Painting Fundamentals
learningObjectives: Explain the four components of paint and how they affect performance
Compare latex and alkyd paints and select appropriate type for application
Select appropriate finish/sheen based on room use and durability requirements
Describe adhesion mechanics and surface energy principles
Apply Climate Zone 6 considerations to painting schedules and product selection

criticalStandards.description: Never paint over surfaces with moisture content above 12% for wood or 15% for drywall
Never use latex paint that has frozen — freeze-thaw destroys the emulsion
Never apply latex paint below 50°F room or surface temperature
Higher sheen paints resist moisture better — use satin or higher in bathrooms and kitchens

content.introduction: Before applying any paint, you need to understand what you're working with and how materials interact with substrates and environmental conditions. This section covers paint composition, finish selection, and the building science that determines whether your work will last.

sections.title: Paint Composition & Performance
sections.body: Paint consists of four primary components. Understanding these helps you select the right product and troubleshoot problems.

subsections.title: The Four Components
subsections.body: Every paint contains pigments, binders, solvents, and additives in varying proportions. Premium paints have higher pigment and binder content; economy paints substitute cheaper fillers.

tables.caption: Paint Components
rows: Higher pigment = better coverage; titanium dioxide is primary white pigment
Hold pigments together, adhere to surface
Determines durability, adhesion, and finish quality; latex (acrylic) vs alkyd (oil)
Water-based (latex) vs solvent-based (alkyd); affects dry time and VOCs
Flow agents, mildewcides, freeze-thaw stabilizers, UV blockers

subsections.title: Latex vs Alkyd
subsections.body: The fundamental choice in paint selection. Premium latex paints now match or exceed alkyd durability for most residential applications. Alkyd's main advantage is leveling (brush marks disappear) and adhesion to difficult surfaces. However, yellowing over time and slow dry times make latex the default choice for walls and ceilings.

sections.title: Finish Types & Applications
sections.body: Sheen affects both appearance and performance. Higher sheen = more durable but shows more imperfections.

subsections.title: Sheen Selection
subsections.body: Match sheen to the room's function and the surface condition.

callouts.content: For walls with imperfections, flat or eggshell hides flaws. For trim that will be cleaned frequently, semi-gloss resists scrubbing. Never use flat paint on trim — it shows every fingerprint.

sections.title: Building Science for Painters
sections.body: Paint failure is often building failure. Understanding moisture, adhesion, and surface dynamics prevents callbacks.

subsections.title: Moisture & Paint
subsections.body: Moisture is the primary cause of paint failure. It can come from substrate moisture (fresh drywall, uncured patches, wet wood), interior humidity (bathrooms, kitchens, humidifiers), vapor drive (moisture migrating through walls from exterior or interior), and condensation (cold surfaces in warm, humid air).

callouts.content: Never paint over surfaces with moisture content above 12% for wood or 15% for drywall. Test with pin meter {{LABS:pin-moisture-meter}} before painting suspect areas.

subsections.title: Adhesion Mechanics
subsections.body: Paint adheres through two mechanisms: mechanical adhesion (paint keys into surface texture) and chemical adhesion (molecular bonding between paint and substrate).

tables.caption: Surface Conditions & Adhesion
rows: Scuff sand or use bonding primer {{LABS:primer-bonding}}
Remove loose material, use penetrating primer

callouts.title: Surface Energy
callouts.content: High surface energy surfaces are easy to paint (water sheets). Low surface energy surfaces resist coating (water beads). If water beads on a surface, paint will too. Either clean the surface or use a bonding primer designed for low-surface-energy substrates.

sections.title: Climate Zone 6 Considerations
sections.body: New Brunswick's climate creates specific challenges for interior painting.

subsections.title: Winter Painting Challenges
subsections.body: Cold weather affects paint application, drying, and film formation in several ways.

tables.caption: Winter Challenges & Solutions
rows: Low humidity from heating, but cold surfaces
Never apply latex below 50°F; warm space 24h before painting
Prime metal immediately, maintain stable temps
Condensation on fresh paint from cold exterior walls
Keep exterior walls warm, avoid painting during extreme cold

callouts.content: Never use latex paint that has frozen. Even if it looks okay, freeze-thaw destroys the emulsion. Paint will fail within months.

callouts.title: Spring Transition
callouts.content: During spring transition, interior surfaces may have condensation from thermal lag. Walls stay cold even as air warms. Test surface temperature — paint won't adhere properly to cold, damp surfaces.

subsections.title: Humidity Swings
subsections.body: Interior humidity in Climate Zone 6 swings from 25-30% (winter) to 60-70% (summer). Winter brings fast drying but risk of poor film formation and cracking. Summer brings slow drying and risk of sags and dust contamination during extended open time.

modules.title: Surface Assessment & Preparation
learningObjectives: Perform comprehensive surface assessment using the five-point protocol
Execute adhesion testing and interpret results
Select appropriate cleaning method based on contamination type
Perform drywall and wood trim repairs to paintable condition
Apply proper sanding technique and dust control

criticalStandards.description: Never paint over mold without killing it first — paint will fail and mold will grow through
Pre-1980 homes may have lead paint — test before sanding, follow RRP requirements if positive

content.introduction: Surface preparation determines 80% of paint job quality. Proper assessment prevents most failures.

procedures.title: Surface Assessment Protocol
procedures.body: Before any prep work, evaluate the entire scope systematically.
steps: Check existing coating: Type (latex/alkyd), condition, adhesion — test adhesion, determine compatibility
Identify surface defects: Cracks, holes, dents, texture damage — document extent, plan repair
Check for contamination: Grease, smoke, water stains, mold — identify type, select cleaning method
Test for moisture: Active leaks, staining, high readings — find source, fix before painting
Evaluate substrate condition: Sound, chalky, peeling, soft — determine if stable enough to coat

procedures.title: Adhesion Testing
procedures.body: The tape test determines if existing coatings will hold or need to be removed.
steps: Make an X-cut through the paint to the substrate using utility knife
Apply strip of masking tape firmly over the X
Pull tape off quickly at 180° angle
Evaluate: Pass (little paint removed), Marginal (some flaking at cut edges), Fail (paint pulls in sheets)
Determine action: Pass = prep and paint over; Marginal = sand/prime, consider bonding primer; Fail = requires full removal or encapsulation

procedures.title: Cleaning Methods
procedures.body: Match the cleaning method to the contamination type.

callouts.content: Never paint over mold without killing it first. Paint will fail and mold will grow through the new coating.

procedures.title: Drywall Repairs
procedures.body: Repair drywall damage before painting. The compound type affects dry time and shrinkage.

tables.caption: Drywall Repair Methods
rows: Reset nail + screw beside it, patch
Drywall scrap, setting then topping compound

callouts.content: Use setting compound for first coat on deep repairs — it doesn't shrink like air-dry compounds. Then topcoat with lightweight for easy sanding.

procedures.title: Sanding Protocol
procedures.body: Sanding creates mechanical adhesion and smooths repairs. Match grit to the task.
steps: Bare wood trim: 120 grit then 150 grit, sand with grain
Previously painted surfaces: 150-220 grit light scuff to break sheen
Drywall patches: 120-150 grit, sand flush, feather edges 6-8 inches
Between coats: 220 grit light scuff only if surface is rough
Vacuum all surfaces with brush attachment after sanding
Wipe with damp cloth or tack cloth, allow to dry before priming

callouts.content: Pre-1980 homes may have lead paint. Test before sanding {{LABS:test-lead}}. If positive, follow RRP (Renovation, Repair, and Painting) rule requirements.

modules.title: Material Handling & Conditioning
learningObjectives: Store and condition paint materials correctly for optimal application
Execute proper paint mixing and boxing procedures
Select and apply paint additives based on conditions
Choose appropriate primer type for specific substrates and conditions

criticalStandards.description: Always box paint for large jobs — switching cans mid-wall creates visible sheen differences
Painting new drywall or patches without primer guarantees flash (sheen difference)

content.introduction: Proper paint handling ensures consistent application and finish quality.

procedures.title: Paint Storage & Conditioning
procedures.body: Temperature affects paint viscosity and application. Cold paint applies poorly; frozen paint is ruined.

procedures.title: Paint Mixing Protocol
procedures.body: Proper mixing ensures consistent color and finish across the job.
steps: Box multiple cans: If using 2+ cans of same color, pour all into large container and mix — color varies slightly between cans
Stir, don't shake: Stirring avoids air bubbles; power-mix only if paint has sat for months
Strain if needed: If any lumps or skin present, strain through mesh {{LABS:strainers-paint}}
Re-stir during use: Every 15-30 minutes, especially with heavy-bodied paints

callouts.content: Always box paint for large jobs. Switching cans mid-wall creates visible sheen differences.

procedures.title: Paint Additives
procedures.body: Additives modify paint performance for specific conditions.

tables.caption: Common Additives
rows: Only if spec'd for spraying; max 10%

callouts.content: In dry winter conditions (25-30% RH), adding Floetrol to latex paint dramatically reduces brush and roller marks by extending open time.

procedures.title: Primer Selection
procedures.body: Primer is not optional paint. It serves specific functions based on substrate and conditions.

tables.caption: Primer Types
rows: Severe stains, smoke damage, odor sealing

callouts.content: Painting new drywall or patches without primer = guaranteed flash (sheen difference where patches show). Always prime first.

modules.title: Planning & Protection
learningObjectives: Plan and execute proper room painting sequence
Set up comprehensive surface protection
Apply masking tape for clean paint lines
Establish proper ventilation and safety measures

criticalStandards.description: Remove tape while paint is still tacky (not wet, not fully dry) for clean edges

content.introduction: Proper planning reduces labor and protects completed work.

procedures.title: Room Sequencing
procedures.body: Paint in sequence that minimizes touch-up and protects completed work.
steps: Paint ceilings first — overspray/drips fall on unpainted surfaces
Paint walls second — work top to bottom
Paint trim last — cleanest edges, less touch-up needed
Within each phase: cut in edges first, then roll/fill
Work from cut edge while wet (maintain wet edge)
Complete full walls or ceiling without stopping

procedures.title: Protection Setup
procedures.body: Protect everything you don't want painted.

tables.caption: Surface Protection
rows: Canvas for durability, plastic underneath on carpet

procedures.title: Tape Application
procedures.body: Proper taping prevents bleed and creates crisp lines.
steps: Verify surface is clean and dry
Apply tape to the surface being protected, not the surface being painted
Press edge firmly — paint bleeds under loose edges
For textured walls: seal tape edge with thin coat of base color first, then apply topcoat
Remove while paint is still tacky, pull at 45° angle away from painted surface

callouts.content: For crisp lines on textured walls, apply tape then seal the edge with a thin coat of the base color. This fills texture gaps. Then apply topcoat color.

modules.title: Brush Technique
learningObjectives: Select appropriate brush type and size for application
Execute proper brush loading and cutting-in technique
Maintain wet edge to prevent lap marks
Properly care for and store brushes

criticalStandards.description: Don't let cut-in edges dry before rolling into them — creates visible picture framing

content.introduction: Brushes are for cutting in, detail work, and areas rollers can't reach. The right brush and technique make the difference between professional and amateur results.

procedures.title: Brush Selection
procedures.body: Match the brush to the paint type and the work.

procedures.title: Cutting-In Procedure
procedures.body: Cutting in creates the border that rollers can't reach. Work in sections to maintain wet edge.
steps: Load brush 1/3 to 1/2 into paint, tap (don't wipe) against container
Apply paint in a strip along the edge, staying 1/4" away from edge initially
Return to edge with less-loaded brush, working paint to the line
Feather the edge away from the corner to blend with roller area
Work in 3-4 foot sections to maintain wet edge
Roll immediately after cutting in (within 10 minutes)

callouts.content: Don't let cut-in edges dry before rolling into them. This creates visible "picture framing" — a different sheen band around edges.

procedures.title: Brush Care
procedures.body: Good brushes are expensive. Proper care extends their life dramatically.
steps: Latex paint: Wrap brush in plastic between coats (up to 24 hours)
Latex paint: Wash thoroughly in water after final coat
Alkyd paint: Wrap in aluminum foil between coats
Alkyd paint: Clean with mineral spirits after final coat
Spin or shake excess water/solvent, reshape bristles
Store hanging or flat, never on bristle tips

modules.title: Roller Technique
learningObjectives: Select appropriate roller cover nap and material for surface type
Execute proper wall and ceiling rolling technique
Maintain wet edge and avoid lap marks
Apply second coats correctly

criticalStandards.description: Never stop mid-ceiling or mid-wall — lap marks are permanent
Wait minimum 2 hours between latex coats (longer in humid conditions)

content.introduction: Rollers are the primary tool for walls and ceilings. Proper technique produces an even coating with no lap marks or roller stipple.

procedures.title: Roller Cover Selection
procedures.body: Nap depth determines how much paint the roller holds and what surface textures it can reach.

procedures.title: Rolling Walls
procedures.body: Use the W or M pattern to distribute paint, then smooth.
steps: Pour paint into tray or bucket with grid
Load roller fully, then roll off excess on grid
Apply in W or M pattern, approximately 3'x3' section
Fill in the pattern without lifting roller
Finish with light vertical strokes, top to bottom, in one direction
Move to adjacent section while maintaining wet edge
Reload and repeat

procedures.title: Rolling Ceilings
procedures.body: Ceiling rolling requires planning to avoid stopping mid-room.
steps: Work in strips parallel to the shorter wall dimension
Roll away from windows (light source) for final strokes
Maintain wet edge across entire strip
Feather at transition to next strip
Move ladders/planks efficiently to maintain pace
Complete entire ceiling without stopping

callouts.content: Never stop mid-ceiling or mid-wall. Lap marks are permanent. Plan your setup to allow continuous application.

procedures.title: Second Coat
procedures.body: Second coat fills in any missed spots and provides full coverage.
steps: Wait minimum 2 hours between coats (check manufacturer spec)
Wait longer in humid conditions (touch test — not tacky)
Light sand or scuff between coats only if surface is rough
Second coat typically requires 20-30% less paint (surface is sealed)
Apply with same technique as first coat

modules.title: Spray Application
learningObjectives: Compare sprayer types and select appropriate equipment
Determine when to spray vs roll based on project conditions
Execute proper spray technique with consistent coverage
Set up adequate overspray protection

criticalStandards.description: Overspray travels 20+ feet — mask thoroughly, seal HVAC returns
Apply two light coats rather than one heavy coat when spraying

content.introduction: Spraying is fastest but requires more setup and protection. When done right, spray finish is smoother than brush or roller. When done wrong, overspray covers everything.

procedures.title: Sprayer Types
procedures.body: Different sprayers for different applications.

procedures.title: When to Spray vs Roll
procedures.body: Spraying isn't always faster when you account for setup and masking.

tables.caption: Spray vs Roll Decision
rows: Small area (setup time exceeds spray time)

procedures.title: Spray Technique
procedures.body: Consistent speed, distance, and overlap produce even coverage.
steps: Test spray pattern on cardboard; adjust pressure/tip until even fan
Maintain 10-12" distance from surface
Keep spray gun perpendicular to surface
Overlap each pass 50%
Move at consistent speed (12-18" per second)
Start and stop spray off the surface when possible
Apply two light coats rather than one heavy coat

callouts.content: Overspray travels far. Mask thoroughly. Seal off HVAC returns. Fine mist settles on everything within 20+ feet of spray zone.

modules.title: Trim & Detail Work
learningObjectives: Execute proper order of operations for trim painting
Apply paint to trim with minimal brush marks
Select and apply appropriate caulk at trim joints
Time caulk application correctly relative to painting

criticalStandards.description: Don't paint latex caulk before it skins (30-60 min) — paint will drag
Don't wait too long after caulking — uncured caulk under paint cracks

content.introduction: Trim requires the highest precision and typically a different sheen than walls. Clean lines and smooth finish separate professional work from DIY.

procedures.title: Trim Painting Order
procedures.body: Order of operations minimizes touch-up and produces clean lines.
steps: Prep all trim first (fill, caulk, sand, prime)
Paint ceilings
Paint walls
Paint trim last (less wall touch-up needed)
For trim: doors/windows first, then base, then crown, then casings

procedures.title: Brush Application for Trim
procedures.body: Trim demands smooth finish with minimal brush marks.
steps: Load brush properly (1/3 into paint)
Apply paint along the length of trim, then spread crossways
Finish with long, light strokes along the grain/length
Work in sections small enough to maintain wet edge
Don't overwork — more strokes = more brush marks
For brush marks: add Floetrol {{LABS:additive-floetrol}}, lay off with very light final strokes

callouts.content: For brush marks on trim, add Floetrol (latex) or Penetrol (alkyd), and lay off with very light final strokes. The paint will self-level as it dries.

procedures.title: Caulking
procedures.body: Caulk fills gaps between trim and wall, and at inside corners. Timing is critical.
steps: Cut tip at 45° angle, small opening (can always make bigger)
Apply consistent bead along joint
Tool immediately with damp finger or caulk tool
Wipe excess with damp rag
Allow to skin over before painting (30-60 minutes)
Apply paint after caulk skins but before full cure (24 hours)

callouts.content: Don't paint latex caulk before it skins. Paint will drag and create a mess. Don't wait too long either — uncured caulk under paint cracks.

modules.title: Touch-up & Repair
learningObjectives: Determine appropriate touch-up approach based on timing
Execute touch-up technique that minimizes visibility
Diagnose common touch-up issues and select solutions
Maintain and store touch-up materials properly

content.introduction: Touch-up is inevitable. Proper technique makes repairs invisible. Poor technique makes them obvious.

procedures.title: Touch-up Timing
procedures.body: The age of the original paint affects how well touch-up will blend.

tables.caption: Touch-up Approach by Timing
rows: Same day, paint still on site
Touch-up may show; feather edges carefully
Touch-up often visible; may need to repaint entire wall

procedures.title: Touch-up Technique
procedures.body: Minimize touch-up visibility with proper technique.
steps: Use same paint (original can if available)
Apply with small brush or mini-roller (match original tool if possible)
Feather edges to blend
Don't overload — multiple light touches better than one heavy
If touch-up flashes (sheen difference), the wall needs a full coat

procedures.title: Touch-up Storage
procedures.body: Keep materials available for future touch-ups.
steps: Keep labeled containers for each job
Note room, color code, and date on container
Store cans upside-down (paint seals lid)
Check periodically, stir before use

callouts.content: Keep labeled touch-up containers for each job. Store upside-down (paint seals lid). Note room, color code, and date.

procedures.title: Common Touch-up Issues
procedures.body: Diagnose problems and select the right solution.

tables.caption: Touch-up Problems & Solutions
rows: Different porosity, old vs new paint

modules.title: Quality Inspection & Defect Prevention
learningObjectives: Perform visual quality inspection using raking light technique
Execute functional tests for adhesion, cure, and washability
Identify common paint defects and their causes
Apply prevention strategies to avoid callbacks

criticalStandards.description: Always photograph substrate conditions and existing defects before painting — protects against callbacks

content.introduction: Quality isn't inspected into a paint job — it's built in. But inspection confirms quality is present and creates documentation that protects everyone.

procedures.title: Visual Inspection
procedures.body: Use raking light (low angle) to reveal defects.

procedures.title: Functional Tests
procedures.body: Beyond visual, test that the paint performs as expected.

procedures.title: Common Defects & Prevention
procedures.body: Understanding defects helps prevent them.

tables.caption: Paint Defects Reference
rows: Proper prep, adhesion test, correct primer
Test moisture, don't paint in direct sun
Over-thinning, thick coats, inadequate dry time
Missed primer, uneven porosity, varying technique
Quality brush, lay off properly, additives
Pressure too high, distance too close, wrong tip

callouts.content: Always photograph substrate conditions and existing defects before painting. This documentation protects against callbacks for pre-existing issues.

productSlugs.category: TSP or degreaser for surface prep
